title: The Middle East and Global Energy Markets – Topics - IEA
gist: IEA's analysis of a 2025-26 Middle East war that halted traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, creating the largest oil supply disruption in market history, spiking Brent over 60%, draining global oil stocks at a record pace, cutting Qatari/UAE LNG, and prompting the IEA's largest-ever 400-million-barrel emergency stock release — with knock-on risks to fertilisers, aluminium and food.

themes.theme: Largest oil supply disruption in history

insights.claim: The war that began 28 February halted traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which carried around 25% of the world's seaborne oil trade in 2025, creating what the IEA's Executive Director called the greatest threat to global energy security in history.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A chokepoint carrying a quarter of seaborne oil being shut is the central tail-risk event for crude pricing and the global economy.
insights.quote: the greatest threat to global energy security in history
insights.locator: The IEA's Executive Director has said

insights.claim: Brent crude peaked at more than 60% above pre-conflict levels in late April before pulling back in late May/early June on strategic stock releases, demand weakness and a US-Iran interim agreement.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Quantifies the geopolitical risk premium (60%+) and identifies stock releases plus diplomacy as the price-relief levers to monitor.
insights.quote: peaking at more than 60% above pre-conflict levels in late April
insights.locator: In the wake of the largest oil supply disruption

insights.claim: Global observed oil stocks fell by 3.8 million barrels per day on average since the war began, with a 143-million-barrel draw in May alone — the fastest inventory depletion on record.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Record-pace stock draws mean limited buffer remains — any prolongation of the disruption removes the main shock absorber holding prices down.
insights.quote: depleted global oil inventories at the fastest pace on record
insights.locator: Even so, supply losses stemming from the Middle East

insights.claim: Even if the US-Iran agreement holds, full recovery in Strait flows is unlikely to be immediate because mines must be cleared from shipping lanes and supply chains need time to normalise.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Physical-clearance lag means a ceasefire does not immediately reprice crude lower — supply tightness persists past any diplomatic breakthrough.
insights.quote: a full recovery in flows is unlikely to be immediate
insights.locator: Yet our latest Oil Market Report

insights.claim: Nearly 3 million barrels per day of Gulf refining capacity has been shut by attacks and lack of export outlets, and jet fuel prices nearly tripled after the loss of Middle Eastern exports while diesel, jet and LPG remain elevated.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Middle-distillate cracks (diesel/jet) are the most exposed trade — refining margins and product spreads, not just crude, carry the disruption.
insights.quote: Nearly 3 million barrels per day of refining capacity in the region has been shut
insights.locator: The Gulf region is a key source of exports

insights.claim: High prices are forcing demand-side adjustment, with the steepest losses in the petrochemical sector from constrained feedstock and aviation activity running well below normal levels.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Petrochemical and aviation demand destruction is the natural price-cap mechanism — watch these sectors as the relief valve on product prices.
insights.quote: the steepest losses have occurred in the petrochemical sector
insights.locator: End users of oil and oil products are curbing consumption

insights.claim: The Strait disruption cut LNG supply from Qatar and the UAE by over 300 million cubic metres per day since 1 March — over 2 bcm of lost gas weekly — with Qatar's Ras Laffan, the world's largest liquefaction facility, offline since a 2 March attack.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Loss of the world's largest LNG plant and ~one-fifth of global LNG trade is a structural supply hole — bullish for global gas and competing supply.
insights.quote: The Ras Laffan facility in Qatar ... has been offline since an attack on 2 March
insights.locator: The Ras Laffan facility in Qatar

insights.claim: The conflict could cause a cumulative loss of around 120 bcm of LNG supply between 2026 and 2030, delaying the anticipated global LNG supply wave, with Dutch TTF standing 35% above pre-war levels by mid-June.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A delayed LNG supply wave reprices the medium-term gas glut thesis — supportive for gas prices and US/other liquefaction economics through 2027.
insights.quote: a cumulative loss of around 120 bcm of LNG supply between 2026 and 2030
insights.locator: The conflict has significantly altered the medium-term outlook

insights.claim: Asian gas prices rose sharply as buyers compete for fewer LNG cargoes, with nearly 90% of Strait LNG volumes destined for Asia and some countries resorting to gas rationing.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Asia bears the concentrated LNG shock — regional price and rationing dynamics there are the leading indicator of global gas tightness.
insights.quote: almost 90% of the total volumes exported via the Strait of Hormuz was destined for the Asian market
insights.locator: In 2025, almost 90% of the total volumes

insights.claim: Only Saudi Arabia and the UAE have operational crude pipelines to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, offering an estimated 3.5-5.5 million b/d of capacity, while Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar and Bahrain rely on the Strait for the vast majority of exports.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Bypass capacity covers only a fraction of Strait volumes — quantifies how little redundancy exists, defining the hard ceiling on crisis mitigation.
insights.quote: Only Saudi Arabia and the UAE have operational crude pipelines that could potentially reroute flows

insights.claim: On 11 March, all IEA member countries unanimously agreed to release 400 million barrels of emergency oil stocks — the largest coordinated release in the agency's history and the sixth ever (after 1991, 2005, 2011 and twice in 2022).
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A 400-million-barrel release sets the upper bound of coordinated supply intervention — a key offset to model against the ongoing stock draw.
insights.quote: making 400 million barrels of emergency oil stocks available – the largest-ever release coordinated by the Agency
insights.locator: On 11 March, IEA Member countries unanimously agreed

insights.claim: Over 30% of global urea trade, ~20% of ammonia and phosphate trade, ~half of global seaborne sulphur trade, and ~5 million tonnes of Gulf aluminium move through the Strait of Hormuz annually, creating food-price and critical-minerals risks beyond energy.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The Strait shock is a multi-commodity event — fertiliser/food and aluminium/critical-minerals supply chains carry second-order inflation risk.
insights.quote: More than 30% of global trade of urea moves through the Strait
